nodes.detail: Entity public statements are weighted as equivalent to entity actions when constructing strategic inferences. Layer 3 builds inferences from announcements, executive quotes, and press releases without discounting them relative to observable resource commitments, on-chain activity, or verified deployments.

telemetry.directional_guidance: For every strategic inference that uses an entity's public statement as evidence, apply a statement-action gap check: is there observable action (resource commitment, on-chain deployment, regulatory filing, financial commitment) that corroborates the statement? If the inference relies solely on statements without corroborating action, classify the inference as STATEMENT_ONLY and reduce its weight by 50%. Do not treat announcements as equivalent to deployments.

nodes.detail: Confidence levels are consistently set higher than the underlying evidence quality supports. The system assigns 'medium' or 'high' confidence to inferences built on behavioral interpretation, single-source data, or unresolved contradictions. This is the most frequent violation across all layers and all runs.

telemetry.directional_guidance: Before assigning any confidence level above 'low', verify: (1) the evidence base includes at least two independent, verifiable sources, (2) no unresolved source contradictions exist in the supporting data, (3) the inference does not rely primarily on behavioral interpretation of a single action. nodes.detail: Forward-looking predictions are given the same evidentiary weight as historical measurements in strategic inferences. Layer 3 treats 'X would happen if Y occurs' with the same confidence as 'X has been measured at value Z'. Predictions about cascade effects, market reactions, and policy responses are weighted as if they were observed facts.

telemetry.directional_guidance: When constructing inferences that mix predictions with measurements, explicitly label each piece of evidence as MEASURED (verified historical data) or PREDICTED (forward-looking expectation). Predictions must carry a confidence discount: an inference built on two measurements is stronger than an inference built on one measurement and one prediction. Never assign an inference higher confidence than its weakest evidentiary input.
